title: CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access / Collision Avoidance) — IEEE 802.11 WiFi
aliases: carrier sense multiple access collision avoidance
examTip: CSMA/CA is used in wireless networks (IEEE 802.11 WiFi) because collision DETECTION (CSMA/CD) is impossible wirelessly — a transmitting node cannot simultaneously receive to detect a collision. Instead, CSMA/CA AVOIDS collisions using: (1) Listen Before Talk — check if channel is idle (DIFS period). (2) Random Backoff — wait a random number of slots if channel is busy. (3) RTS/CTS handshake — optional, reserve the channel before transmitting. (4) ACK — receiver sends ACK to confirm receipt; no ACK means collision occurred.

blocks.label: Start: Node has a Frame to Transmit
Backoff Counter: Decrement each IDLE slot. Freeze counter when channel goes BUSY. Resume when IDLE again.
Optional: Send RTS (Request to Send) frame
Received CTS (Clear to Send) from Destination?
No CTS → Double CW (Congestion Window) and Retry
Transmit the Full Data Frame
blocks.note: Entire frame sent — no collision detection during transmit

blocks.label: Wait for ACK (SIFS period before ACK expected)
No ACK → Collision assumed. Double CW. Retry from Backoff.
✅ Transmission Successful!
blocks.note: Reset CW to CWmin. Channel released.

blocks.label: NAV — Network Allocation Vector
sublabels: All OTHER nodes hearing the RTS or CTS set their NAV (virtual carrier sense timer).
They defer transmission until NAV expires.

blocks.label: CSMA/CA vs CSMA/CD
sublabels: CSMA/CD (Collision Detection) — Ethernet (IEEE 802.3): Detects collisions during transmission. If signal strength doubles → collision detected → jam signal sent → exponential backoff. WIRED networks only — need simultaneous TX+RX capability.

CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance) — WiFi (IEEE 802.11): Cannot detect collisions wirelessly (transmitter can't hear its own signal reflected). Instead AVOIDS collisions using: DIFS wait, random backoff, RTS/CTS, ACK confirmation.
Key difference: CD = detect & recover. CA = prevent & confirm. CA has higher overhead but is necessary for wireless.

theory:
CSMA/CA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance) is the MAC protocol used in IEEE 802.11 (WiFi) wireless networks. Unlike Ethernet's CSMA/CD, collision detection is not possible in wireless because a transmitting node cannot simultaneously listen to detect a collision on the channel.

CSMA/CA Protocol Steps:
1. Listen Before Talk (LBT): Before transmitting, the node senses the channel for DIFS (Distributed Coordination Function Interframe Space, ~50µs). If the channel is idle for the full DIFS period, proceed to step 2.
2. Random Backoff: If the channel was busy during DIFS, wait a random backoff time (random number × slot time) from a Contention Window [0, CWmin]. The backoff counter decrements only when the channel is idle; it pauses if the channel becomes busy.
3. RTS/CTS Handshake (optional): Send an RTS (Request to Send) frame. The destination responds with CTS (Clear to Send) after SIFS (Short Interframe Space, ~10µs). Other nodes hearing RTS or CTS update their NAV (Network Allocation Vector) and defer — this solves the hidden node problem.
4. Transmit: Send the complete data frame.
5. Wait for ACK: After SIFS, the receiver sends an ACK frame. If no ACK arrives within ACK_Timeout → collision assumed → double the Contention Window → retry with new backoff.
6. Success: Reset CW to CWmin. The channel is released.

Hidden Node Problem: Node A and C cannot hear each other, but both can reach B. Without RTS/CTS, both might transmit to B simultaneously (collision at B). RTS/CTS forces A and C to hear the handshake and set their NAV.
keyPoints: CSMA/CA used in IEEE 802.11 WiFi — collision AVOIDANCE, not detection (wireless can't do CSMA/CD).
DIFS wait + random backoff prevents simultaneous transmissions — avoidance strategy.
RTS (Request to Send) / CTS (Clear to Send) optional handshake solves the hidden node problem.
ACK confirms receipt; no ACK = collision assumed → exponential backoff (double CW).
NAV (Network Allocation Vector) = virtual carrier sense — nodes defer when they hear RTS or CTS.
SIFS < DIFS: High-priority ACK/CTS can preempt lower-priority data frame transmissions.
useCases: Understanding why wireless networks have higher overhead than wired Ethernet.
Designing and analyzing wireless network performance under heavy traffic loads.
